narrative_blocks.body: In 1797, scarcely fifteen years old, Horace Cowles was admitted to the freshman class at Yale College. A growing disability forced him to leave in his senior year before examinations; invited by the faculty to attend with his class, he declined and was never enrolled as a graduate. He became a merchant with his brother, then justice of the peace, selectman, town clerk, state legislator, choir instructor, and member of the Missionary, Temperance, Anti-Masonic, and Anti-Slavery Societies.

narrative_blocks.header: One of three Mende girls from the Amistad
narrative_blocks.body: After the Amistad captives were freed, the Mende from that ship spent eight months in Farmington while arrangements were made for their return to Sierra Leone. Horace Cowles took in one of the three young Mende girls — believed to be Teme, also called Tamie — who attended school with the other Mende each morning and learned domestic skills with the Cowles children. Horace fell ill toward the end of the Mende's stay and died in 1841; his son Samuel Smith inherited the house and continued the Underground Railroad work.

open_questions.question: Is Rev. Noah Porter Sr. (1781-1866), who preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, a relation to the Hooker tree?
open_questions.notes: Noah Porter Sr. was minister of the First Church of Christ in Farmington 1806-1866 (60 years). He preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, published as a 26-page pamphlet. His son Noah Porter Jr. (1811-1892) became president of Yale. The Porter family is prominent in Farmington history — worth checking whether there is a Hooker or Cowles family connection. (Cowles Genealogy #642)

narrative_blocks.body: Lucy died in 1809 at the age of four. Lucy’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.
Lucy died in 1812 at the age of two.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the Hooker family descent through grandfather Lt.-Col. Isaac Cowles's wife Lucina Hooker (H00309).

narrative_blocks.body: Ann died in 1813 at the age of one.

burial.plot_notes: Tombstones to both his wives and four children at Farmington.

narrative_blocks.body: In 1838, Samuel Smith Cowles began editing and printing the Charter Oak, an anti-slavery newspaper in Hartford, CT. He had learned the printer's trade at 17 in Windsor, Vermont, worked as a journeyman printer in Boston, and brought the abolitionist press to Hartford before returning to Farmington in 1843. He served as judge of the probate court, justice of the peace, member of the Legislature, and secretary-treasurer of the Farmington Savings Bank.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited his father's Underground Railroad station
narrative_blocks.body: When Horace Cowles died in 1841, Samuel Smith Cowles inherited 27 Main Street and continued his father's work hiding fugitive slaves in the house. His family had already sheltered one of the young Mende girls from the Amistad during the captives' eight-month stay in Farmington after the trial. Samuel went further: he edited the Charter Oak, Hartford's anti-slavery newspaper, and published several anti-slavery books.

narrative_blocks.header: Sat at the door all day
narrative_blocks.body: As a young girl, Mary Ann Cowles was left in charge of her family's Farmington home while her parents were out. A slave catcher from the South came looking for a fugitive hidden inside. She sat at the front door all day and refused to let anyone enter — the slave catcher had to leave empty-handed.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Atwood Hardy of Haverhill MA

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale Theological School. In business NYC, Pittsburgh PA, then California.

narrative_blocks.body: Theodore died in 1859 at the age of two. Theodore’s sibling James died the same season — the family likely lost both children to one illness.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Solomon Cowles of Farmington CT

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary lieutenant-colonel; Farmington farmer and tavern-keeper

military_service.pension: File No. 9153, U.S. Pension Office. Pensioned.
military_service.notes: Served 4th Sgt, 1st Co., Col. Hooker's 15th CT Militia, May 1776-May 1785. Jul 1776: went with company to New York under Capt. John Porter; Battle of Long Island; returned to Kingsbridge. Appointed quartermaster-sergeant by QM Jonathan Bull; served to close of the war. At Danbury Alarm; at New Haven (BTL018); at Battle of Saratoga (BTL011). Pensioned. Pension File No. 9153.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought from Long Island to Saratoga
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Cowles rose from sergeant to lieutenant-colonel commandant of the 15th Connecticut Militia during the Revolution. He saw action at the Battle of Long Island, the Danbury and New Haven alarms, and Saratoga.

narrative_blocks.header: Afterward he kept a Farmington tavern
narrative_blocks.body: Back home in Farmington, Cowles farmed his father's homestead and kept a tavern, prospering into a man of local standing. A federal pension file recorded his long Revolutionary service.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Isaac Cowles

notable.notable_blurb: Capt., CT Militia; innkeeper; 'Solomon Cowles and Sons'

narrative_blocks.header: Fined for drinking East India tea
narrative_blocks.body: On March 23, 1775, Solomon Cowles and his wife Martha were 'convicted of partaking of that detestable and obnoxious vegetable called East India tea' — forced to sign a public confession printed in the Connecticut Courant. Yet by 1774 Cowles had already served on the committee to collect provisions for the relief of Boston's inhabitants, and in 1775 receipted for supplies furnished to the Rifle Battalion marching to the American camp near Boston. He was a patriot despite the tea.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose to captain of the Farmington trainband
narrative_blocks.body: Cowles was commissioned an ensign in the Farmington trainband in 1759 and made captain in the colony's first regiment two years later. He also kept an inn on the town's main street and ran the firm Solomon Cowles and Sons.

parents.mother_research_notes: Eunice Hollister Seymour (dau. of Capt. Stephen and Abigail Treat Hollister; b. 1696 Wethersfield CT)

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut

burialCemetery.name: Memento Mori Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Samuel Hooker buried here; no stone survives.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
